lead: Every smooth move we have ever been part of began with the same quiet truth: the first few steps matter most. Get those right and the rest tends to look after itself. Since 2005 our family-run team near Ipswich has watched countless Suffolk households begin their moves, and the difference between a calm one and a chaotic one is almost always decided in the opening weeks. So before you reach for a single box, here are the genuine first steps that set a move up to go well.

sections.h: Pin Down Your Likely Moving Date
sections.body:
Almost everything in a move hangs off the date, so that is where any sensible plan begins. You may not have a firm completion yet, and that is perfectly normal, but you can usually pencil in a likely window. Even a rough fortnight to aim for gives you something to plan around and stops the whole process drifting along with no shape to it.

Once you have that window, you can start to see the shape of the weeks ahead. We have found that the families who settle on an approximate date early tend to feel far calmer than those who keep everything vague in the hope of avoiding commitment. A date, however provisional, turns a worry into a plan, and a plan is something you can actually work through.

Do remember to build in a little flexibility, particularly if you are part of a chain. Solicitors get busy, surveys throw up questions, and dates shift more often than anyone would like. If your whole plan assumes everything lands exactly on time, a single delay unravels it. Leave yourself some slack from the very start and the inevitable wobbles become minor rather than alarming.

sections.h: Set a Budget You Can Actually Live With
sections.body:
Moving costs creep up quietly if you let them, so an early, honest look at the numbers pays off. Beyond the removals itself there are solicitors, surveys, possibly stamp duty, the cost of any new furniture and the small but steady drip of packing materials and bits and pieces. Writing it all down in one place stops any single item taking you by surprise later.

When it comes to the move itself, decide early how much you are willing to spend versus how much work you are happy to take on yourself. Packing your own boxes saves money but costs evenings; handing the whole job to us costs more but buys back your time. Neither is wrong. The point is to know your own answer before you start ringing round, so the choices you make all pull in the same direction.

A clear budget also makes the survey conversation far easier. When we come to look at your move, you can tell us plainly what matters most to you, and we can shape the service to fit rather than guessing. In our experience the customers who feel best about their move are simply the ones who decided early what they wanted and what they were prepared to pay for it.

sections.h: Take Stock of What You Actually Own
sections.body:
Long before any packing begins, it is worth walking through your home with fresh eyes and getting a real sense of how much there is. Most people own far more than they think, and the loft, the garage and the shed are where the surprises always hide. A slow walk round, room by room, tells you the true scale of the job and feeds directly into everything that follows.

As you go, start mentally sorting things into what is coming with you, what could go to a charity shop or the tip, and what you are genuinely unsure about. You do not have to act on any of it yet; the aim at this stage is simply to understand the load. A house that feels manageable in your head is far less daunting than one you have never properly looked at.

This early stock-take has a practical payoff too. The clearer a picture you have of your possessions, the more accurately we can quote when the time comes, and the easier it is to judge whether you will need storage for any overlap between properties. Knowing what you own is the quiet foundation that every other decision rests on.

sections.h: Research and Approach a Removals Firm
sections.body:
With a date in mind and a sense of the load, the next step is to find the people who will actually move you. This is worth a little care, because a removals firm is not just a van; it is a team you are trusting with everything you own on one of the more stressful days of your life. Look for an established, settled firm with proper local knowledge rather than the cheapest name you can find.

Local knowledge matters more than people expect. A team that knows Ipswich, the surrounding villages and the wider Suffolk lanes will navigate tight access, awkward parking and rural drives far more smoothly than one passing through. Since 2005 we have learned the quirks of countless local properties, and that hard-won familiarity is exactly the sort of thing worth asking about when you make contact.

When you do reach out, arrange a survey rather than settling for a rough phone figure. A proper look around lets us judge the volume, check access at both ends and give you a quote you can rely on. It is also your chance to get a feel for the firm and ask the questions that matter to you. Booking early, once you are happy, secures your date before the busy slots fill up.

Boxes are the visible part of a move, but the paperwork is what quietly catches people out, so it pays to begin the admin sooner than feels necessary. Make a list of everyone who needs your new address: the bank, the council, the DVLA, your insurers, your employer, your GP, and the long tail of subscriptions and accounts that only surface when post stops arriving.

Arranging to redirect your post is a sensible early move, as it acts as a safety net for anything you inevitably forget. Around the same time, note which utility suppliers serve both your current and future homes, and plan to take meter readings at both ends. We always suggest photographing every meter on the day with the date showing; it takes seconds and settles any billing dispute before it can start.

If you have children at school or pets registered with a vet, those transfers can take time, so flagging them early avoids a last-minute scramble. None of this is difficult, but it adds up, and it is precisely the sort of background task that turns into a panic if left too late. Spread gently across the opening weeks, it barely registers and leaves you free to focus on the move itself.

sections.h: Build a Simple Plan You Can Follow
sections.body:
The final first step is to gather everything you have decided into one simple plan. This does not need to be elaborate; a single sheet on the fridge with the big jobs spread across the weeks is plenty. The whole purpose is to stop everything landing on you at once in the final few days, which is when otherwise calm moves tend to come unstuck.

Work backwards from your likely date and slot the major tasks into the weeks before it: book the removals, declutter, sort the admin, then pack room by room starting with the spaces you use least. In our experience, the families who break the job into small weekly pieces sail through, while those who leave it as one enormous looming task find it grows heavier the longer they ignore it.

Keep the plan flexible and forgiving. Tick things off as you go, shuffle them when dates move, and do not panic if a week slips. A plan is a guide, not a cage. Get these first steps in place, lean on a local team you trust, and you will find that the move ahead feels far less like a mountain and far more like a series of small, manageable jobs done in good time.

faqs: What is the very first thing I should do when planning a move?

Pin down a likely moving date, even a provisional one. Almost everything else hangs off it, and a rough window gives the whole process shape. We have found the families who settle on an approximate date early feel far calmer than those who keep everything vague.
How early should I start planning a house move?

The sooner the better, but six to eight weeks before your likely completion is a sensible point to begin the serious planning. That leaves time to budget, book a removals team, sort the admin and pack at a steady pace rather than cramming it all into the final stressful days.
Do I need to budget for more than just the removals?

Yes. Alongside the move itself there are solicitors, surveys, possibly stamp duty, new furniture and a steady drip of packing materials. Writing it all down in one place early stops any single cost taking you by surprise and makes the survey conversation far easier.
Should I declutter before I start packing?

It helps enormously to at least take stock first. Walk through your home, loft, garage and shed included, and get a real sense of what you own. There is no point paying to move things you no longer want, and a clearer picture lets us quote more accurately too.
